([/ˈskoʊki/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); formerly **Niles Center**) is a [village](/wiki/Village_(United_States) "Village (United States)") in [Cook County, Illinois](/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois "Cook County, Illinois"), United States. According to the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), its population was 67,824.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Skokie lies approximately 15 miles (24 km) north of [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago")'s downtown Loop. The name Skokie comes from a [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi_language "Potawatomi language") word for 'marsh'.[[4]](#cite_note-4) For many years, Skokie promoted itself as "The World's Largest Village".[[5]](#cite_note-5) Skokie's streets, like that of many suburbs, are largely a continuation of the [Chicago street grid](/wiki/Roads_and_expressways_in_Chicago "Roads and expressways in Chicago"), and the village is served by the [Chicago Transit Authority](/wiki/Chicago_Transit_Authority "Chicago Transit Authority"), further cementing its connection to the city.

**Skokie** ([/ˈskoʊki/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); formerly **Niles Center**) is a [village](/wiki/Village_(United_States) "Village (United States)") in [Cook County, Illinois](/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois "Cook County, Illinois"), United States. According to the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), its population was 67,824.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Skokie lies approximately 15 miles (24 km) north of [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago")'s downtown Loop. The name Skokie comes from a [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi_language "Potawatomi language") word for 'marsh'.[[4]](#cite_note-4) For many years, Skokie promoted itself as "The World's Largest Village".[[5]](#cite_note-5) Skokie's streets, like that of many suburbs, are largely a continuation of the [Chicago street grid](/wiki/Roads_and_expressways_in_Chicago "Roads and expressways in Chicago"), and the village is served by the [Chicago Transit Authority](/wiki/Chicago_Transit_Authority "Chicago Transit Authority"), further cementing its connection to the city.

Skokie was originally a [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans")-[Luxembourger](/wiki/Luxembourger "Luxembourger") farming community, but was later settled by a sizeable [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") population, especially after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). At its peak in the mid-1960s, nearly 60% of the population was [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews"), the largest proportion of any Chicago suburb.[[6]](#cite_note-Smilk-6) Skokie still has many Jewish residents (now about 30% of the population) and over a dozen synagogues.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) It is home to the [Illinois Holocaust Museum and Education Center](/wiki/Illinois_Holocaust_Museum_and_Education_Center "Illinois Holocaust Museum and Education Center"), which opened in northwest Skokie in 2009.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Skokie has twice received national attention for court cases decided by the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court"). In the mid-1970s, it was at the center of *[National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie](/wiki/National_Socialist_Party_of_America_v._Village_of_Skokie "National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie")*, in which a Nazi group, backed by the [American Civil Liberties Union](/wiki/American_Civil_Liberties_Union "American Civil Liberties Union"), invoked the [First Amendment](/wiki/First_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "First Amendment to the United States Constitution") in an attempt to schedule a Nazi rally in Skokie.[[10]](#cite_note-10) At the time, Skokie had a significant population of [Holocaust survivors](/wiki/Holocaust_survivors "Holocaust survivors"). Skokie ultimately lost that case, though the rally was never held.[[11]](#cite_note-SkokievNzs-11)

### Beginnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Beginnings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Summer_2006_0882.jpg/250px-Summer_2006_0882.jpg)](/wiki/File:Summer_2006_0882.jpg)

A 1925 "Chicago"-style bungalow in Skokie

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Skokie_Village_Hall.JPG/250px-Skokie_Village_Hall.JPG)](/wiki/File:Skokie_Village_Hall.JPG)

Skokie Village Hall

In 1888, the community was incorporated as Niles Centre.[[12]](#cite_note-12) About 1910, the spelling was [Americanized](/wiki/American_and_British_English_spelling_differences "American and British English spelling differences") to "Niles Center". However, the name caused postal confusion with the neighboring village of [Niles](/wiki/Niles,_Illinois "Niles, Illinois"). A village-renaming campaign began in the 1930s. In a referendum on November 15, 1940, residents chose the Native American name "Skokie" over the name "Devonshire".

During the real estate boom of the 1920s, large parcels were subdivided; many two- and three-flat apartment buildings were built, with the "Chicago"-style [bungalow](/wiki/Bungalow#Chicago_bungalow "Bungalow") a dominant architectural specimen. Large-scale development ended as a result of the [Great Crash of 1929](/wiki/Wall_Street_Crash_of_1929 "Wall Street Crash of 1929") and consequent [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). It was not until the 1940s and the 1950s, when parents of the [baby boom](/wiki/Baby_boom "Baby boom") generation moved their families out of Chicago, that Skokie's housing development began again. Consequently, the village developed commercially, an example being the Old Orchard Shopping Center, currently named [Westfield Old Orchard](/wiki/Westfield_Old_Orchard "Westfield Old Orchard").

During the night of November 27–28, 1934, after a gunfight in nearby [Barrington](/wiki/Barrington,_Illinois "Barrington, Illinois") that left two [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") agents dead, two accomplices of notorious 25-year-old bank-robber [Baby Face Nelson](/wiki/Baby_Face_Nelson "Baby Face Nelson") (Lester Gillis) dumped his bullet-riddled body in a ditch along Niles Center Road adjoining the St. Peter Catholic Cemetery,[[13]](#cite_note-13) a block north of Oakton Street in the town.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The first African-American family to move to Skokie arrived in 1961, and [open-housing](/wiki/Open_Housing_Act "Open Housing Act") activists helped to integrate the suburb subsequently.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Name")]

Historic maps named the Skokie marsh as *Chewab Skokie*, a probable derivation from *Kitchi-wap choku*, a [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi") term meaning 'great marsh'.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Other Indigenous names include *skoutay* or *scoti*, an Algonquian words for 'fire'.[[17]](#cite_note-17) "Skokie Marsh" was used by local botanists, notably [Henry Chandler Cowles](/wiki/Henry_Chandler_Cowles "Henry Chandler Cowles"), as early as 1901.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The village name was changed from "Niles Center" to "Skokie" by referendum in 1940.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The name change may also have been influenced by James Foster Porter, a Chicago resident, who had explored the "Skoki Valley" in [Banff National Park](/wiki/Banff_National_Park "Banff National Park") in Canada in 1911 and admired the name; Porter supported the name "Skokie" in the referendum.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Jewish community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Jewish community")]

Skokie attracted Jewish residents as newcomers did not face the same level of hostility as they did in some other Chicago suburbs, where it wasn't supported to sell property to Jewish institutions.[[21]](#cite_note-reich-21) In the post-World War II real-estate developers and builders that were often Jewish themselves, advertised Skokie in the older, urban Jewish neighborhoods in the South, West and North Sides of Chicago.[[21]](#cite_note-reich-21) New arrivals were also drawn to the urban planning vision of the Skokie Villager Master Plan of 1946, with its focus on fostering single-family homes rather than apartment living.[[21]](#cite_note-reich-21) By 1975, Jewish residents made up 57% of the suburb's population.[[21]](#cite_note-reich-21)

An estimated 8,000 Jewish [Holocaust survivors](/wiki/Holocaust_survivors "Holocaust survivors") settled in Skokie in the 1950s, 1960s and 1970s, making up 10% of the Jewish community in 1978.[[21]](#cite_note-reich-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish") was widely spoken and the area developed a Jewish character with synagogues, Hebrew Schools, Jewish delis, [kosher](/wiki/Kosher "Kosher") butchers, Israeli bakeries and [Judaica](/wiki/Judaica "Judaica") stores.[[21]](#cite_note-reich-21) Survivors mostly socialized among each other.[[21]](#cite_note-reich-21)

In 1964, Joseph Neumann, a Skokie resident and survivor of [Auschwitz](/wiki/Auschwitz "Auschwitz"), provided testimony at the trial of one of the concentration camp's guards.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In early 1960's, [Skokie Valley Traditional synagogue](/wiki/Skokie_Valley_Agudath_Jacob_Synagogue "Skokie Valley Agudath Jacob Synagogue") was one of four synagogues in the Chicago area that was attacked.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The synagogue, now known as Skokie Valley Agudath Jacob Synagogue, has become the largest Orthodox Jewish congregation in Chicago.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 1962, the [Jewish Reconstructionist Federation](/wiki/Jewish_Reconstructionist_Federation "Jewish Reconstructionist Federation") held its annual conference in the suburb.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In 1970, Dr. Korczak Terrace was dedicated in Skokie, honoring a [Polish Jewish](/wiki/Polish_Jewish "Polish Jewish") martyr, [Janusz Korczak](/wiki/Janusz_Korczak "Janusz Korczak").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In 1987, Skokie unveiled a bronze Holocaust memorial statute.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Supreme Court rulings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Supreme Court rulings")]

Twice in its history, Skokie has been the focal point of cases before the United States Supreme Court. *[National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie](/wiki/National_Socialist_Party_of_America_v._Village_of_Skokie "National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie")*, [432 U.S. 43](/wiki/Case_citation "Case citation") (1977), involved a [First Amendment](/wiki/First_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "First Amendment to the United States Constitution") issue. *[Solid Waste Agency of Northern Cook County (SWANCC) v. U.S. Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/Solid_Waste_Agency_of_Northern_Cook_County_v._Army_Corps_of_Engineers "Solid Waste Agency of Northern Cook County v. Army Corps of Engineers")*, 531 U.S. 159 (2001) touched upon the [Commerce Clause](/wiki/Commerce_Clause "Commerce Clause").

#### *National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie")]

Main article: [National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie](/wiki/National_Socialist_Party_of_America_v._Village_of_Skokie "National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie")

In 1977 and 1978, Illinois neo-Nazis of the [National Socialist Party of America](/wiki/National_Socialist_Party_of_America "National Socialist Party of America") (NSPA) attempted to hold a march in Skokie, far from their headquarters on Chicago's south side. Originally, the neo-Nazis had planned a political rally in [Marquette Park](/wiki/Marquette_Park_(Chicago) "Marquette Park (Chicago)") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"). The park is located in what was then a predominantly all-white neighborhood, similar to the [situation in 1966](/wiki/Marquette_Park_(Chicago)#Civil_rights_protests "Marquette Park (Chicago)"), when a crowd of 4,000 Marquette Park residents gathered to watch [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") lead a march, some waving Confederate flags or throwing bottles, bricks and rocks at the protesters; King was knocked to his knees when struck by a rock.[[30]](#cite_note-Wilkerson-30) However, the Chicago authorities thwarted the NSPA's plans.[[11]](#cite_note-SkokievNzs-11)

Seeking another free-speech political venue, the NSPA group chose to march on Skokie. Given the many [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") survivors living in Skokie, the village's government thought the Nazi march would be disruptive, and refused the NSPA permission to hold the event. The NSPA appealed that decision, and the [American Civil Liberties Union](/wiki/American_Civil_Liberties_Union "American Civil Liberties Union") interceded on their behalf, in *[National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie](/wiki/National_Socialist_Party_of_America_v._Village_of_Skokie "National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie")*. An Illinois appeals court raised the injunction issued by a Cook County Circuit Court judge, ruling that the presence of the [swastika](/wiki/Swastika "Swastika"), the Nazi emblem, would constitute deliberate provocation of the people of Skokie. However, the Court also ruled that Skokie's attorneys had failed to prove that either the Nazi uniform or their printed materials, which it was alleged that the Nazis intended to distribute, would incite violence.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Moreover, because Chicago subsequently lifted its Marquette Park political demonstration ban, the NSPA ultimately held its rally in Chicago. The attempted Illinois Nazi march on Skokie was dramatized in the television film *[Skokie](/wiki/Skokie_(film) "Skokie (film)")* in 1981. It was satirized in the film *[The Blues Brothers](/wiki/The_Blues_Brothers_(film) "The Blues Brothers (film)")* in 1980.

#### Migratory bird rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Migratory bird rule")]

See also: [Solid Waste Agency of Northern Cook County v. Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/Solid_Waste_Agency_of_Northern_Cook_County_v._Army_Corps_of_Engineers "Solid Waste Agency of Northern Cook County v. Army Corps of Engineers")

In 2001, the decision by Skokie and 22 other communities belonging to the Solid Waste Agency of Northern Cook County to use an isolated [wetland](/wiki/Wetland "Wetland") as a solid waste disposal site resulted in a lawsuit. Ultimately, the case went all the way to the United States Supreme Court, and resulted in an overturn of the federal [migratory bird rule](/wiki/Migratory_bird_rule "Migratory bird rule").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the 2010 census, Skokie has a total area of 10.06 square miles (26.06 km2), all land.[[32]](#cite_note-census-g001-32) The village is bordered by [Evanston](/wiki/Evanston,_Illinois "Evanston, Illinois") to the east, [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") to the southeast and southwest, [Lincolnwood](/wiki/Lincolnwood,_Illinois "Lincolnwood, Illinois") to the south, [Niles](/wiki/Niles,_Illinois "Niles, Illinois") to the southwest, [Morton Grove](/wiki/Morton_Grove,_Illinois "Morton Grove, Illinois") to the west, [Glenview](/wiki/Glenview,_Cook_County,_Illinois "Glenview, Cook County, Illinois") to the northwest, and [Wilmette](/wiki/Wilmette,_Illinois "Wilmette, Illinois") to the north.

The village's street circulation is a street-grid pattern, with a major east–west thoroughfare every half mile: Old Orchard Road, Golf Road, Church Street, Dempster Street, Main Street, Oakton Street, Howard Street, and Touhy Avenue. The major north–south thoroughfares are Skokie Boulevard, [Crawford Avenue](/wiki/Crawford_Avenue "Crawford Avenue"), and McCormick Boulevard; the major diagonal streets are [Lincoln Avenue](/wiki/Lincoln_Avenue_(Chicago) "Lincoln Avenue (Chicago)"), Niles Center Road, East Prairie Road and Gross Point Road.

Skokie's north–south streets continue the street names and (house number) grid values of Chicago's north—south streets—with the notable exceptions of [Cicero Avenue](/wiki/Cicero_Avenue "Cicero Avenue"), which is renamed [Skokie Boulevard](/wiki/Skokie_Highway "Skokie Highway") within Skokie, and Chicago's Pulaski Road retains its original Chicago City name, Crawford Avenue. The east–west streets continue Evanston's street names, but with Chicago grid values, such that Evanston's Dempster Street is 8800 north in Skokie addresses.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

Skokie is in the Hot-summer humid continental climate, or [Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") Dfa zone.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The zone includes four distinct seasons. Winter is cold with snow. Spring warms up with precipitation and storms, some of which can be severe and include tornadoes. Summer has high precipitation and storms. Fall cools down.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[40]](#cite_note-40) there were 67,824 people, 22,503 households, and 16,206 families residing in the village. The population density was 6,739.27 inhabitants per square mile (2,602.05/km2). There were 25,256 housing units at an average density of 2,509.54 per square mile (968.94/km2). The racial makeup of the village was 51.36% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 7.94% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.48% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 27.78% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.05% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 4.61% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 7.78% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 10.59% of the population.

There were 22,503 households, out of which 32.5% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 55.68% were married couples living together, 11.23% had a female householder with no husband present, and 27.98% were non-families. 25.48% of all households were made up of individuals, and 16.28% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 3.37 and the average family size was 2.78.

The village's age distribution consisted of 23.3% under the age of 18, 6.7% from 18 to 24, 22.2% from 25 to 44, 27.6% from 45 to 64, and 20.0% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 42.9 years. For every 100 females, there were 89.0 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 85.5 males.

The median income for a household in the village was $74,725, and the median income for a family was $93,491. Males had a median income of $46,915 versus $37,025 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the village was $37,827. About 7.5% of families and 9.7% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 12.9% of those under age 18 and 8.8% of those age 65 or over.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Economy")]

The village's AAA [bond rating](/wiki/Bond_rating "Bond rating") attests to strong economic health via prudent fiscal management. In 2003, Skokie became the first municipality in the United States to achieve nationally accredited police, fire, and public works departments, and a Class-1 fire department, per the [Insurance Services Office](/wiki/Insurance_Services_Office "Insurance Services Office") (ISO) ratings. Likewise, in 2003 *[Money](/wiki/Money_(magazine) "Money (magazine)")* magazine named Skokie one of the 80 fastest-growing suburbs in the U.S.

Besides strong manufacturing and retail commerce bases, Skokie's economy will add [health sciences](/wiki/Health_sciences "Health sciences") jobs; in 2003, [Forest City Enterprises](/wiki/Forest_City_Enterprises "Forest City Enterprises") announced their re-development of the vacant [Pfizer](/wiki/Pfizer "Pfizer") research laboratories, in downtown Skokie, as the Illinois Science + Technology Park, a 23-acre (93,000 m2) campus of research installations—2 million square feet (190,000 m2) of chemistry, genomics, toxicology laboratories, clean rooms, NMR suites, conference rooms, etc.). In 2006, NorthShore University HealthSystem announced installing their consolidated data center operations at the park, adding 500 jobs to the economy. Map maker [Rand McNally](/wiki/Rand_McNally "Rand McNally") is also headquartered in Skokie. More recently, the village has focused heavily on the revitalization of both the downtown and [central business districts](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district"), incorporating [Transit Oriented Development](/wiki/Transit-oriented_development "Transit-oriented development") principles in the process.

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[Westfield Old Orchard](/wiki/Westfield_Old_Orchard "Westfield Old Orchard"), an [upscale shopping](/wiki/Luxury_goods "Luxury goods") center, is one of the country's first and is the third largest mall by total square footage in Illinois.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Skokie-Orchard-Fountain.jpg/250px-Skokie-Orchard-Fountain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skokie-Orchard-Fountain.jpg)

Fountain at Westfield Old Orchard

The Skokie Northshore Sculpture Park is situated along the [North Shore Channel](/wiki/North_Shore_Channel "North Shore Channel") between Dempster Street and Touhy Avenue on the east side of McCormick Boulevard. The first sculptures were built in the park in 1988 and it now has over 70 sculptures. Three areas are toured May through October of each year, on the last Sunday of the month with a presentation by a [docent](/wiki/Museum_docent "Museum docent").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Just north of the sculpture garden is a statue to [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi") with five of his famous quotations engraved around the base. This was dedicated on October 2, 2004.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In addition to municipally-managed public spaces, the village is also home to the North Shore Center for the Performing Arts, encompassing Centre East, Northlight Theatre and the [Skokie Valley Symphony Orchestra](http://www.svso.org). The facility celebrated its 20th anniversary in 2016.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

The [Illinois Holocaust Museum and Education Center](/wiki/Illinois_Holocaust_Museum_and_Education_Center "Illinois Holocaust Museum and Education Center") in Skokie

The [Illinois Holocaust Museum and Education Center](/wiki/Illinois_Holocaust_Museum_and_Education_Center "Illinois Holocaust Museum and Education Center") opened in Skokie on April 19, 2009.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Skokie's founding and early days were the subject of the 2023 documentary, *Holy Ground*.

### Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Library")]

On October 7, 2008, the [Skokie Public Library](/wiki/Skokie_Public_Library "Skokie Public Library") received the 2008 [National Medal for Museum and Library Service](/wiki/National_Medal_for_Museum_and_Library_Service "National Medal for Museum and Library Service"),[[52]](#cite_note-52) notably for its cultural programming and multilingual services.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/North_Shore_Center_for_Performing_Arts.jpg/250px-North_Shore_Center_for_Performing_Arts.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_Shore_Center_for_Performing_Arts.jpg)

North Shore Center for Performing Arts in Skokie

The Skokie Park District maintains public spaces and historical sites within its more than 240 acres (0.97 km2) of parkland and in its ten facilities.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The [Skokie Valley Trail](/wiki/Skokie_Valley_Trail "Skokie Valley Trail") is a multi-use trail connecting the northwest side of Chicago to the communities of [Lincolnwood](/wiki/Lincolnwood,_Illinois "Lincolnwood, Illinois") and Skokie.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) In 2023, the Village announced plans to extend the Valley Line Trail from its current terminus at [Dempster-Skokie Station](/wiki/Dempster%E2%80%93Skokie_station "Dempster–Skokie station") to its northernmost boundary at Old Orchard Road. The result will be a continuous trail from the [City of Chicago](/wiki/City_of_chicago "City of chicago") to the northern suburbs beyond Skokie. The project is estimated to be completed by 2025.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

The [North Shore Channel Trail](/wiki/North_Shore_Channel_Trail "North Shore Channel Trail") also passes through town.

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Skokie,_Illinois&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

The [Chicago "L"s](/wiki/Chicago_%22L%22 "Chicago \"L\"") [Yellow Line](/wiki/Yellow_Line_(CTA) "Yellow Line (CTA)") terminates at the [Dempster Street](/wiki/Dempster%E2%80%93Skokie_(CTA_station) "Dempster–Skokie (CTA station)") station in Skokie. Construction has been completed on a new Yellow Line train station at [Oakton Street](/wiki/Oakton%E2%80%93Skokie_(CTA_station) "Oakton–Skokie (CTA station)"), to serve downtown Skokie. It opened on April 30, 2012.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Additionally, the CTA is commissioning an alternatives analysis study on the extension of the Yellow Line terminal to Old Orchard Road for [Federal Transit Administration](/wiki/Federal_Transit_Administration "Federal Transit Administration") New Start grants.[[64]](#cite_note-64)
The New Starts program allows federal funds to be used for [capital projects](/wiki/Capital_expenditure "Capital expenditure") provided that all extensions for a given problem (i.e., enabling easy transportation for reverse commuters to [Westfield Old Orchard](/wiki/Westfield_Old_Orchard "Westfield Old Orchard")) are considered. The extension recommended by the CTA, is the elevation of the Yellow Line to a new terminal south of Old Orchard Road. This extension was canceled.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Although the Yellow Line is the fastest transportation to and from the city, the village also is served by [CTA](/wiki/Chicago_Transit_Authority "Chicago Transit Authority") and [Pace](/wiki/Pace_(transit) "Pace (transit)") bus routes. The [Pace Pulse](/wiki/Pace_Pulse "Pace Pulse") Dempster Line opened through the village in 2023, which provides a high-frequency limited stop bus service between [O'Hare Airport](/wiki/O%27Hare_International_Airport "O'Hare International Airport") and the city of Evanston with sheltered stops adjacent to the Yellow Line. However, [Greyhound Bus](/wiki/Greyhound_Bus "Greyhound Bus") service to the Dempster Street train station has been discontinued. For automobile transport, [Interstate 94](/wiki/Interstate_94 "Interstate 94"), the [Edens Expressway](/wiki/Edens_Expressway "Edens Expressway"), traverses western Skokie, with interchanges at Touhy Avenue, Dempster Street, and Old Orchard Road.
